steps.title: The Framework of Electrical Rules
steps.objective: Explanation A
keyTerms: HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act)
keyIdeas: In the electrical industry, we do not guess how to work safely. We follow a strict framework of rules designed to prevent injury and death. These rules are divided into two main groups: Statutory and Non-statutory.

Statutory means it is a law passed by Parliament. If you break a statutory regulation, you are committing a criminal offense, which can lead to prosecution, heavy fines, or even imprisonment. It is the 'Law of the Land.'

Non-statutory refers to industry standards and guidance. While these are not laws themselves, they are the 'Industry Rulebook' we use to prove we are working to a safe, professional standard. Think of statutory as the 'must-do' legal requirement and non-statutory as the 'how-to' technical guide. Both are essential for a competent electrician to understand and follow.
Explanation A
anchorFacts: In the electrical industry, we do not guess how to work safely. We follow a strict framework of rules designed to prevent injury and death. These rules are divided into two main groups: Statutory and Non-statutory.

Statutory means it is a law passed by Parliament. If you break a statutory regulation, you are committing a criminal offense, which can lead to prosecution, heavy fines, or even imprisonment. It is the 'Law of the Land.'

Non-statutory refers to industry standards and guidance. While these are not laws themselves, they are the 'Industry Rulebook' we use to prove we are working to a safe, professional standard. Think of statutory as the 'must-do' legal requirement and non-statutory as the 'how-to' technical guide. Both are essential for a competent electrician to understand and follow.
misconceptionsToWatch: Statutory is the law; non-statutory is the professional standard.

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is it technically incorrect for an electrician to treat non-statutory regulations as optional suggestions?
deeperAnswerGuidance: They are the industry rulebook; They define the professional standard; They are used to prove a safe standard of work; They are essential for a competent person
steps.hint: Statutory is the law; non-statutory is the professional standard.

steps.title: How Law and Guidance Work Together
steps.objective: Explanation B
keyIdeas: A common mistake is thinking that because something is 'non-statutory,' it is optional and can be ignored. In reality, these two types of regulations work together in a legal setting.

If an accident occurs and you end up in court, a judge will first look at the statutory law, such as the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), to see what your legal duty was. They will then look at the non-statutory guidance, such as BS 7671 (The Wiring Regulations), to see if you followed the industry-standard way of working.

If you ignored the guidance without a valid technical reason, the court will likely decide you failed your legal duty. Therefore, following non-statutory guidance is the primary way an electrician proves they have met their statutory legal obligations. They are not just suggestions; they are the benchmark for safety.

steps.title: Statutory Regulations: The Law
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.
keyTerms: Health and Safety at Work Act
keyIdeas: Statutory regulations are mandatory. You have no choice but to follow them. The primary piece of legislation is the Health and Safety at Work Act, often shortened to HASAWA. This is the 'Umbrella Act' that covers every person in every workplace, placing duties on both employers and employees.

Underneath this umbrella, we have more specific laws. The most critical for us is the Electricity at Work Regulations, known as EAWR. This law demands that electrical systems are designed, constructed, and maintained safely to prevent danger. Other statutory examples include PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations), which ensures your tools are safe, and the PPE (Personal Protective Equipment at Work) Regulations. Because these are statutory, failing to comply is a criminal act.

steps.title: Non-statutory Regulations: The Standards
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.
keyIdeas: Non-statutory regulations are technical standards. The most famous example is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations or 'The Big Blue Book.' This document is the national standard for electrical installations in the UK. It tells us exactly how to design and install systems to remain safe.

Other non-statutory documents include the On-Site Guide, which is a simplified handbook for common, small-scale tasks, and various Guidance Notes. We also use Codes of Practice and the Unite Union Book. These documents provide the practical, step-by-step instructions on how to be a 'competent person.' While you won't be arrested simply for not owning a copy of BS 7671, failing to follow its technical rules usually means you have failed to meet the legal safety requirements set out in the EAWR.

steps.title: Identifying Specific Statutory Laws
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: 203.LO1.AC1.1.
keyTerms: Control of Substances Hazardous to Health
keyIdeas: To work safely, you must identify which documents are statutory laws. If the title includes 'Act' or 'Regulations' passed by the government, it is statutory. Key examples include HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act) and EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations).

You must also recognize other specific laws: COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) deals with dangerous chemicals like cleaning fluids or lead. CDM (Construction Design and Management) manages safety and roles on building sites. PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) covers the safety of your drills and testers. Finally, the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations requires employers to carry out risk assessments. If the document is a legal mandate that can lead to a court case, it is categorized as statutory.

steps.title: Worked Example: Categorizing Site Documentation
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to distinguish between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a real-world scenario.
keyIdeas: Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are starting a new job at a construction site. Your supervisor hands you a folder containing several documents: the Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), BS 7671 (The Wiring Regulations), and the On-Site Guide. You need to know which of these are legally mandatory 'Statutory' regulations and which are 'Non-statutory' guidance.

Step 1: Identify the Statutory Regulations. Look for documents passed by Parliament that carry the weight of law. In this folder, HASAWA is the 'Umbrella Act' for all workplace safety, and EAWR is the specific law governing electrical work. These are statutory; failing to follow them is a criminal offense.

Step 2: Identify the Non-statutory Guidance. Look for technical standards and handbooks that explain how to achieve safety. BS 7671 (The Wiring Regulations) is the national standard, and the On-Site Guide is a simplified version for common tasks. While these are not laws themselves, they are the 'Industry Rulebook' used to prove you have met the legal requirements of the EAWR.
Final Result: You categorize HASAWA and EAWR as Statutory (The Law), and BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide as Non-statutory (The Standard).
Takeaway: Statutory regulations like EAWR tell you what you MUST achieve by law; non-statutory guidance like BS 7671 tells you HOW to achieve it technically to stay out of court.

terms.simpleDefinition: Laws passed by Parliament that are mandatory; failure to follow them is a criminal offense.
terms.misconception: Thinking these are just 'best practice' rather than legal requirements that can lead to prosecution.

terms.simpleDefinition: Industry standards and guidance that are not law but are used to show you have met legal requirements.
terms.misconception: Believing that because they aren't law, they are optional and can be ignored safely.

terms.term: HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act)
terms.simpleDefinition: The primary UK law governing health and safety in every workplace.
terms.misconception: Thinking it only applies to the boss; it actually places duties on every employee too.

terms.simpleDefinition: Statutory regulations that specifically focus on electrical safety in the workplace.
terms.misconception: Confusing these legal requirements with the technical rules found in BS 7671.

terms.simpleDefinition: The UK national standard for electrical installations, also known as the Wiring Regulations.
terms.misconception: Thinking BS 7671 is a statutory law; it is actually a non-statutory standard.

terms.simpleDefinition: A non-statutory handbook that simplifies BS 7671 for common, small-scale electrical tasks.
terms.misconception: Thinking the On-Site Guide covers every complex industrial scenario; it is for basic installations only.

terms.simpleDefinition: Non-statutory documents that give practical instructions on how to work safely and meet legal duties.
terms.misconception: Thinking they are just suggestions; they are often used in court to prove what a 'competent person' should have done.

teachChecks.title: The Framework of Electrical Rules
retrievalTextLines: In the electrical industry, we do not guess how to work safely. We follow a strict framework of rules designed to prevent injury and death. These rules are divided into two main groups: Statutory and Non-statutory.

Statutory means it is a law passed by Parliament. If you break a statutory regulation, you are committing a criminal offense, which can lead to prosecution, heavy fines, or even imprisonment. It is the 'Law of the Land.'

Non-statutory refers to industry standards and guidance. While these are not laws themselves, they are the 'Industry Rulebook' we use to prove we are working to a safe, professional standard. Think of statutory as the 'must-do' legal requirement and non-statutory as the 'how-to' technical guide. Both are essential for a competent electrician to understand and follow.
teachChecks.hint: Statutory is the law; non-statutory is the professional standard.

teachChecks.title: How Law and Guidance Work Together
retrievalTextLines: A common mistake is thinking that because something is 'non-statutory,' it is optional and can be ignored. In reality, these two types of regulations work together in a legal setting.

If an accident occurs and you end up in court, a judge will first look at the statutory law, such as the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), to see what your legal duty was. They will then look at the non-statutory guidance, such as BS 7671 (The Wiring Regulations), to see if you followed the industry-standard way of working.

If you ignored the guidance without a valid technical reason, the court will likely decide you failed your legal duty. Therefore, following non-statutory guidance is the primary way an electrician proves they have met their statutory legal obligations. They are not just suggestions; they are the benchmark for safety.
teachChecks.hint: Following the industry standard (non-statutory) is how you prove you followed the law (statutory).

teachChecks.title: Statutory Regulations: The Law
teachChecks.objective: Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.
retrievalTextLines: Statutory regulations are mandatory. You have no choice but to follow them. The primary piece of legislation is the Health and Safety at Work Act, often shortened to HASAWA. This is the 'Umbrella Act' that covers every person in every workplace, placing duties on both employers and employees.

Underneath this umbrella, we have more specific laws. The most critical for us is the Electricity at Work Regulations, known as EAWR. This law demands that electrical systems are designed, constructed, and maintained safely to prevent danger. Other statutory examples include PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations), which ensures your tools are safe, and the PPE (Personal Protective Equipment at Work) Regulations. Because these are statutory, failing to comply is a criminal act.
teachChecks.hint: HASAWA and EAWR are examples of statutory laws that must be followed.

teachChecks.title: Non-statutory Regulations: The Standards
teachChecks.objective: Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.
retrievalTextLines: Non-statutory regulations are technical standards. The most famous example is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations or 'The Big Blue Book.' This document is the national standard for electrical installations in the UK. It tells us exactly how to design and install systems to remain safe.

Other non-statutory documents include the On-Site Guide, which is a simplified handbook for common, small-scale tasks, and various Guidance Notes. We also use Codes of Practice and the Unite Union Book. These documents provide the practical, step-by-step instructions on how to be a 'competent person.' While you won't be arrested simply for not owning a copy of BS 7671, failing to follow its technical rules usually means you have failed to meet the legal safety requirements set out in the EAWR.
teachChecks.hint: BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide are non-statutory industry standards.

teachChecks.title: Identifying Specific Statutory Laws
retrievalTextLines: To work safely, you must identify which documents are statutory laws. If the title includes 'Act' or 'Regulations' passed by the government, it is statutory. Key examples include HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act) and EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations).

You must also recognize other specific laws: COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) deals with dangerous chemicals like cleaning fluids or lead. CDM (Construction Design and Management) manages safety and roles on building sites. PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) covers the safety of your drills and testers. Finally, the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations requires employers to carry out risk assessments. If the document is a legal mandate that can lead to a court case, it is categorized as statutory.
teachChecks.hint: Look for 'Act' or 'Regulations' in the name, such as COSHH, CDM, or EAWR.

        "In the electrical industry, we do not guess how to work safely. We follow a strict framework of rules designed to prevent injury and death. These rules are divided into two main groups: Statutory and Non-statutory.",

        "Non-statutory refers to industry standards and guidance. While these are not laws themselves, they are the 'Industry Rulebook' we use to prove we are working to a safe, professional standard. Think of statutory as the 'must-do' legal requirement and non-statutory as the 'how-to' technical guide. Both are essential for a competent electrician to understand and follow."

integrative.title: Integrative Close: Law vs. Standard in Practice
integrative.objective: Synthesize the relationship between statutory law and non-statutory guidance in a professional legal context.
retrievalTextLines: Statutory regulations like the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) set the mandatory legal duty to ensure safety.
Non-statutory guidance like BS 7671 (The Wiring Regulations) provides the technical standards and 'how-to' instructions for the industry.
In a legal setting, following the non-statutory standard is the primary method used to prove that you have fulfilled your statutory legal obligations.
integrative.questionText: A client argues that because BS 7671 is 'non-statutory,' it is merely a suggestion and they don't want to pay for the extra testing it requires. Explain the professional and legal relationship between BS 7671 and the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) to justify why the work must follow the 'Big Blue Book.'
answerGuidance: BS 7671 is the non-statutory technical standard used to prove compliance with the statutory EAWR.
While BS 7671 isn't a law itself, following it is the recognized way to meet the legal safety requirements of the Electricity at Work Regulations.
If an accident occurs, a court would use BS 7671 as the benchmark to decide if the electrician met their statutory duty under the EAWR.
Failing to follow BS 7671 would likely be seen as a breach of the statutory EAWR in a court of law.
integrative.hint: Think about which document is the 'Law of the Land' and which one is the 'Industry Rulebook' used to prove you've followed that law.

steps.title: The Framework of Electrical Rules
steps.objective: Explanation A
keyTerms: HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act)
keyIdeas: In the electrical industry, we do not guess how to work safely. We follow a strict framework of rules designed to prevent injury and death. These rules are divided into two main groups: Statutory and Non-statutory.

Statutory means it is a law passed by Parliament. If you break a statutory regulation, you are committing a criminal offense, which can lead to prosecution, heavy fines, or even imprisonment. It is the 'Law of the Land.'

Non-statutory refers to industry standards and guidance. While these are not laws themselves, they are the 'Industry Rulebook' we use to prove we are working to a safe, professional standard. Think of statutory as the 'must-do' legal requirement and non-statutory as the 'how-to' technical guide. Both are essential for a competent electrician to understand and follow.
Explanation A
anchorFacts: In the electrical industry, we do not guess how to work safely. We follow a strict framework of rules designed to prevent injury and death. These rules are divided into two main groups: Statutory and Non-statutory.

Statutory means it is a law passed by Parliament. If you break a statutory regulation, you are committing a criminal offense, which can lead to prosecution, heavy fines, or even imprisonment. It is the 'Law of the Land.'

Non-statutory refers to industry standards and guidance. While these are not laws themselves, they are the 'Industry Rulebook' we use to prove we are working to a safe, professional standard. Think of statutory as the 'must-do' legal requirement and non-statutory as the 'how-to' technical guide. Both are essential for a competent electrician to understand and follow.
misconceptionsToWatch: Statutory is the law; non-statutory is the professional standard.

basicQuestions.questionText: What term is used to describe a law that has been passed by Parliament?
What type of offense is committed if a person breaks a statutory regulation?
What term describes the industry standards and technical guides used by electricians?
answerGuidance: HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act)

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is it technically incorrect for an electrician to treat non-statutory regulations as optional suggestions?
deeperAnswerGuidance: They are the industry rulebook; They define the professional standard; They are used to prove a safe standard of work; They are essential for a competent person
steps.hint: Statutory is the law; non-statutory is the professional standard.

steps.title: How Law and Guidance Work Together
steps.objective: Explanation B
keyIdeas: A common mistake is thinking that because something is 'non-statutory,' it is optional and can be ignored. In reality, these two types of regulations work together in a legal setting.

If an accident occurs and you end up in court, a judge will first look at the statutory law, such as the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), to see what your legal duty was. They will then look at the non-statutory guidance, such as BS 7671 (The Wiring Regulations), to see if you followed the industry-standard way of working.

If you ignored the guidance without a valid technical reason, the court will likely decide you failed your legal duty. Therefore, following non-statutory guidance is the primary way an electrician proves they have met their statutory legal obligations. They are not just suggestions; they are the benchmark for safety.

steps.title: Statutory Regulations: The Law
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.
keyTerms: Health and Safety at Work Act
keyIdeas: Statutory regulations are mandatory. You have no choice but to follow them. The primary piece of legislation is the Health and Safety at Work Act, often shortened to HASAWA. This is the 'Umbrella Act' that covers every person in every workplace, placing duties on both employers and employees.

Underneath this umbrella, we have more specific laws. The most critical for us is the Electricity at Work Regulations, known as EAWR. This law demands that electrical systems are designed, constructed, and maintained safely to prevent danger. Other statutory examples include PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations), which ensures your tools are safe, and the PPE (Personal Protective Equipment at Work) Regulations. Because these are statutory, failing to comply is a criminal act.

steps.title: Non-statutory Regulations: The Standards
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.
keyIdeas: Non-statutory regulations are technical standards. The most famous example is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations or 'The Big Blue Book.' This document is the national standard for electrical installations in the UK. It tells us exactly how to design and install systems to remain safe.

Other non-statutory documents include the On-Site Guide, which is a simplified handbook for common, small-scale tasks, and various Guidance Notes. We also use Codes of Practice and the Unite Union Book. These documents provide the practical, step-by-step instructions on how to be a 'competent person.' While you won't be arrested simply for not owning a copy of BS 7671, failing to follow its technical rules usually means you have failed to meet the legal safety requirements set out in the EAWR.

steps.title: Identifying Specific Statutory Laws
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: 203.LO1.AC1.1.
keyTerms: Control of Substances Hazardous to Health
keyIdeas: To work safely, you must identify which documents are statutory laws. If the title includes 'Act' or 'Regulations' passed by the government, it is statutory. Key examples include HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act) and EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations).

You must also recognize other specific laws: COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) deals with dangerous chemicals like cleaning fluids or lead. CDM (Construction Design and Management) manages safety and roles on building sites. PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) covers the safety of your drills and testers. Finally, the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations requires employers to carry out risk assessments. If the document is a legal mandate that can lead to a court case, it is categorized as statutory.

steps.title: Worked Example: Categorizing Site Documentation
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to distinguish between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a real-world scenario.
keyIdeas: Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are starting a new job at a construction site. Your supervisor hands you a folder containing several documents: the Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), BS 7671 (The Wiring Regulations), and the On-Site Guide. You need to know which of these are legally mandatory 'Statutory' regulations and which are 'Non-statutory' guidance.

Step 1: Identify the Statutory Regulations. Look for documents passed by Parliament that carry the weight of law. In this folder, HASAWA is the 'Umbrella Act' for all workplace safety, and EAWR is the specific law governing electrical work. These are statutory; failing to follow them is a criminal offense.

Step 2: Identify the Non-statutory Guidance. Look for technical standards and handbooks that explain how to achieve safety. BS 7671 (The Wiring Regulations) is the national standard, and the On-Site Guide is a simplified version for common tasks. While these are not laws themselves, they are the 'Industry Rulebook' used to prove you have met the legal requirements of the EAWR.
Final Result: You categorize HASAWA and EAWR as Statutory (The Law), and BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide as Non-statutory (The Standard).
Takeaway: Statutory regulations like EAWR tell you what you MUST achieve by law; non-statutory guidance like BS 7671 tells you HOW to achieve it technically to stay out of court.
